title: No Previous Error Code / Normal Operation
shortDescription: A single blink indicates the system has no stored fault codes and is operating normally or in standby.
fullDescription: On many Lennox furnace models, a single LED blink indicates that no diagnostic fault codes are stored. The system is either in normal standby or has recently been reset. If the furnace is not heating, the issue may be in the thermostat or wiring rather than the furnace itself.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company. Always ensure carbon monoxide detectors are installed and functional.

causes.cause: System recently reset or power cycled
Thermostat wiring issue preventing heat call

symptoms: Single LED blink on control board
Furnace in standby mode
No active heating cycle
System appears normal but not running

fixSteps.title: Check Thermostat
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the thermostat is set to HEAT mode and the set temperature is above room temperature.

fixSteps.title: Raise Temperature Setting
fixSteps.instruction: Increase the thermostat set point 5 degrees above room temperature to trigger a heat call.

fixSteps.title: Check Thermostat Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: At the furnace, verify the thermostat wires are securely connected to the correct terminals on the control board.

fixSteps.title: Test with Jumper
fixSteps.instruction: Briefly jump R and W terminals on the control board. If the furnace starts, the thermostat or its wiring is the issue.

whenToCallPro: If the furnace does not respond to a thermostat call and jumper test fails to start the system.

faq.q: Is one blink an error on my Lennox furnace?
faq.a: No, on most Lennox models one blink means no fault codes are stored. It indicates normal standby operation.

faq.q: Why is my Lennox furnace not starting with 1 blink?
faq.a: One blink means the furnace control board is OK. Check your thermostat settings, batteries, and wiring.

faq.q: Should I call for service with 1 blink?
faq.a: Not typically. One blink is not an error. Only call if the furnace fails to start when the thermostat calls for heat.

title: System Lockout — Retries Exceeded
shortDescription: The furnace has locked out after failing to ignite within the allowed number of retries.
fullDescription: Two LED blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate the system has locked out due to failed ignition attempts. The control board attempted to ignite the burners multiple times without success. The lockout will automatically reset after approximately one hour, or can be manually reset by cycling power.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company. Do not repeatedly attempt to reset the furnace if it continues to lock out, as this may indicate a gas or ignition system issue.

causes.cause: Dirty or failing hot surface igniter
Dirty flame sensor not detecting flame
Gas supply issue or closed gas valve

symptoms: Two LED blinks on control board
Furnace not producing heat
System attempted ignition but failed
Burners may have briefly lit then gone out
Lockout lasts about one hour before auto-reset

fixSteps.title: Reset the Furnace
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace power switch, wait 30 seconds, and turn it back on. Observe the ignition sequence.

fixSteps.title: Clean the Flame Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the flame sensor (thin metal rod in the burner area). Remove it and gently clean with fine emery cloth or steel wool. Reinstall.

fixSteps.title: Inspect the Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Check the igniter for cracks. During the ignition cycle, it should glow bright orange/white. If it glows dimly or not at all, replacement is needed.

fixSteps.title: Verify Gas Supply
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm the manual gas shutoff valve on the supply line is fully open. Check that other gas appliances work.

whenToCallPro: If the furnace locks out repeatedly after cleaning the flame sensor and checking the igniter, or if you suspect a gas supply issue.

faq.q: How long does a Lennox lockout last?
faq.a: The auto-reset lockout typically lasts about one hour. You can manually reset sooner by cycling the furnace power off and on.

faq.q: Why does my Lennox furnace keep locking out?
faq.a: Repeated lockouts usually indicate a dirty flame sensor, failing igniter, or gas supply issue. Clean the flame sensor first as it is the most common cause.

faq.q: How do I clean a Lennox flame sensor?
faq.a: Turn off the furnace, remove the single screw holding the flame sensor, and gently clean the metal rod with fine emery cloth. Do not use sandpaper or anything abrasive.

title: Pressure Switch Error
shortDescription: The pressure switch did not close or opened unexpectedly during operation.
fullDescription: Three blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate a pressure switch fault. Either the switch failed to close after the draft inducer started, or it opened during the heating cycle. This safety device verifies proper venting before and during combustion.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Never bypass the pressure switch. It ensures combustion gases are properly vented. If you smell gas, evacuate and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Blocked exhaust or intake vent pipe
Cracked or disconnected pressure switch hose

symptoms: Three LED blinks on control board
Draft inducer runs but burners do not light
Furnace starts then shuts down mid-cycle
Clicking sound from pressure switch area

fixSteps.title: Check Vent Pipes Outside
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the exhaust and intake PVC pipes on the exterior of your home. Remove any ice, snow, bird nests, leaves, or debris blocking the openings.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Pressure Hose
fixSteps.instruction: Check the small rubber tube connecting the pressure switch to the draft inducer housing. Ensure both ends are connected and the hose has no cracks or water inside.

fixSteps.title: Clear Condensate Drain
fixSteps.instruction: On high-efficiency Lennox furnaces, check the condensate drain trap and line for clogs. Flush with warm water.

fixSteps.title: Test Pressure Switch
fixSteps.instruction: With the inducer running, check for continuity across the pressure switch with a multimeter. It should close (show continuity) when the inducer is running.

whenToCallPro: If vent pipes are clear, hose is intact, and the pressure switch still does not close. The draft inducer motor or switch may need replacement.

faq.q: Why does this happen in cold weather?
faq.a: Freezing temperatures can cause ice to form in the exhaust vent pipe, blocking airflow and triggering the pressure switch error.

faq.q: Can I bypass the pressure switch to get heat?
faq.a: Absolutely not. The pressure switch ensures combustion gases vent outside. Bypassing it risks carbon monoxide buildup in your home.

faq.q: How much does a pressure switch cost?
faq.a: A Lennox pressure switch costs $25-$60 for the part. Professional replacement runs $150-$300 total.

title: Open High Limit or Auxiliary Limit Switch
shortDescription: A temperature limit switch has tripped due to overheating.
fullDescription: Four blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate that a high limit or auxiliary limit switch has opened due to excessive temperature. The furnace overheated and the safety switch shut down the burners to prevent damage to the heat exchanger.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Repeated overheating can crack the heat exchanger, leading to potential carbon monoxide leakage. Ensure CO detectors are working. If the limit switch trips repeatedly, stop using the furnace until the cause is found.

causes.cause: Blocked return or supply air vents
Blower motor failure or weak capacitor

symptoms: Four LED blinks on control board
Furnace runs briefly then shuts off
Very hot air from vents before shutdown
Burning smell from furnace area
Furnace short-cycles repeatedly

fixSteps.title: Replace Air Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Check and replace the furnace air filter immediately. A clogged filter is the number one cause of overheating.

fixSteps.title: Open All Vents
fixSteps.instruction: Walk through your home and ensure all supply and return vents are fully open and not blocked by furniture or curtains.

fixSteps.title: Check Blower Operation
fixSteps.instruction: Set the thermostat fan to ON (continuous). The blower should run. If it does not, the blower motor or capacitor may be faulty.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Blower Wheel
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off power and inspect the blower wheel for heavy dust or debris buildup. A dirty blower wheel significantly reduces airflow.

whenToCallPro: If the filter is clean, vents are open, and the furnace still overheats. The blower motor, capacitor, or heat exchanger may need inspection.

faq.q: Can I reset the limit switch?
faq.a: Some Lennox limit switches auto-reset when the furnace cools down. Others have a manual reset button. The switch resets, but the underlying cause must still be addressed.

faq.q: How often should I change the filter?
faq.a: Check monthly, replace every 1-3 months for standard filters. If you have pets or allergies, check more frequently.

faq.q: Is overheating dangerous?
faq.a: Yes, repeated overheating can crack the heat exchanger, which can leak carbon monoxide. Address the cause promptly and ensure CO detectors are working.

title: Flame Sensed When No Flame Should Be Present
shortDescription: The flame sensor detects flame when the gas valve should be closed.
fullDescription: Five blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate that the flame sensing circuit is detecting a flame signal when the gas valve has not been energized. This is a safety lockout condition that could indicate a leaking gas valve or a wiring fault in the flame sensor circuit.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL: This code may indicate a gas valve that is not fully closing. If you smell gas, evacuate immediately and call 911 or your gas company. Do not attempt DIY repair of this condition.

causes.cause: Gas valve not closing fully (internal leak)
Flame sensor wire shorted to ground
Faulty control board flame sensing circuit

symptoms: Five LED blinks on control board
Furnace locked out and will not start
Possible gas odor (evacuate if detected)
Error occurs before ignition sequence begins

fixSteps.title: Check for Gas Odor
fixSteps.instruction: Carefully check for any gas smell near the furnace. If detected, leave immediately and call emergency services.

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off furnace power and gas valve. Wait 5 minutes. Restore gas, then power. If the code returns immediately, do not attempt further troubleshooting.

fixSteps.title: Check Flame Sensor Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: With power off, inspect the flame sensor wire for damage or places where it may be touching the furnace chassis (grounding out).

fixSteps.title: Call for Professional Service
fixSteps.instruction: This code requires professional diagnosis. A technician will test the gas valve, flame sensing circuit, and control board.

whenToCallPro: Immediately. This is a safety-critical code that requires professional diagnosis and repair.

faq.q: Is this code dangerous?
faq.a: Potentially yes. It may indicate a gas valve that is not fully closing. Have it inspected by a professional promptly.

faq.q: Can I fix this myself?
faq.a: No. Gas valve and flame sensing circuit issues require professional repair for safety.

faq.q: How much will repair cost?
faq.a: If a gas valve replacement is needed, expect $200-$800 depending on the model and labor costs in your area.

title: Line Voltage Polarity or Ground Fault
shortDescription: Incorrect wiring polarity or missing ground detected.
fullDescription: Six blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate the control board has detected reversed line voltage polarity or a grounding issue. Correct polarity is essential for the flame sensing circuit to work properly.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off power at the breaker before inspecting electrical connections. Incorrect wiring can be a shock hazard.

causes.cause: Hot and neutral wires reversed at furnace
Polarity reversed at the breaker panel or outlet
Control board polarity detection circuit fault

symptoms: Six LED blinks on control board
Furnace will not operate
May occur after recent electrical work
Flame sensor fails to detect flame if polarity incorrect

fixSteps.title: Turn Off Power
fixSteps.instruction: Switch off the furnace breaker before inspecting any wiring.

fixSteps.title: Check Polarity at Furnace
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the hot (black) and neutral (white) wires are connected correctly at the furnace power junction. Use a voltage tester.

fixSteps.title: Verify Ground
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the ground wire is securely connected to the furnace chassis and runs back to the panel ground bus.

fixSteps.title: Test Outlet Polarity
fixSteps.instruction: If the furnace plugs into an outlet, use an outlet tester to verify correct polarity and grounding.

whenToCallPro: If you are not comfortable working with electrical wiring or if correcting polarity does not resolve the error.

faq.q: Why does my furnace need correct polarity?
faq.a: The flame sensing circuit requires proper polarity to detect the flame. With reversed polarity, the sensor cannot sense the flame, causing ignition failures.

faq.q: Did my electrician wire it wrong?
faq.a: It is possible. If this code appeared after recent electrical work, have the wiring checked.

faq.q: What tool do I need to check polarity?
faq.a: An outlet tester ($10-15) or a multimeter can verify polarity. Both are available at hardware stores.

title: Gas Valve or Igniter Circuit Fault
shortDescription: The control board detected an issue with the gas valve or igniter electrical circuit.
fullDescription: Seven blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate an electrical fault in the gas valve or igniter circuit. The control board cannot properly energize these components for safe ignition.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Do not attempt to manually open or adjust the gas valve. If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company.

causes.cause: Loose wiring at gas valve or igniter

symptoms: Seven LED blinks on control board
No ignition attempt
Draft inducer may run but igniter does not glow
Furnace locked out

fixSteps.title: Check Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the hot surface igniter for visible cracks. Test resistance with a multimeter (should read 10-200 ohms).

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure all wire connectors at the igniter and gas valve are firmly seated with no corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Test Gas Valve Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance across the gas valve coil terminals. Compare to spec (typically 10-60 ohms). An open reading indicates a failed coil.

fixSteps.title: Replace Failed Component
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the igniter or gas valve as indicated by testing. Gas valve replacement should be done by a licensed technician.

whenToCallPro: For gas valve replacement or if testing indicates a control board fault.

faq.q: Can I replace the igniter myself?
faq.a: Yes, the igniter is a common DIY repair. Use the exact OEM part number for your Lennox model. Do not touch the element with bare hands.

faq.q: Is this the same as a lockout?
faq.a: It is different from a lockout. This indicates a circuit fault rather than failed ignition attempts. The system detects the fault before attempting ignition.

faq.q: How much does a Lennox igniter cost?
faq.a: OEM Lennox igniters typically cost $30-$80. Universal replacements are less expensive but may not fit perfectly.

title: Igniter Circuit Open
shortDescription: The hot surface igniter circuit is open, indicating a broken igniter element.
fullDescription: Eight blinks on a Lennox furnace specifically indicate that the hot surface igniter has an open circuit — the heating element is broken or has a fracture that prevents current flow. The igniter cannot heat up to ignite the gas.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off the furnace before handling the igniter. The igniter is fragile and reaches extremely high temperatures during operation.

causes.cause: Igniter damaged during filter change or maintenance

symptoms: Eight LED blinks on control board
Igniter does not glow at all
Draft inducer runs but no ignition occurs
Furnace shuts down after inducer pre-purge

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace and visually inspect the igniter for cracks. Even hairline cracks can cause an open circuit.

fixSteps.title: Check Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the igniter wire connector is firmly seated. Wiggle the connection to check for intermittent contact.

fixSteps.title: Test Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the igniter and measure resistance. A good igniter reads 10-200 ohms. OL (open line) confirms it is broken.

fixSteps.title: Replace Igniter
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the mounting screw, disconnect the old igniter, install the new one. Handle only by the ceramic base — never touch the element.

whenToCallPro: If you are not comfortable accessing the burner compartment or if a new igniter does not resolve the issue.

faq.q: How do I know which igniter to buy?
faq.a: Use your Lennox model number (found on the data plate inside the furnace) to order the exact OEM igniter. Cross-reference part numbers online.

faq.q: Can I use a universal igniter?
faq.a: Universal igniters may work but the mounting bracket and connector may not match perfectly. OEM parts are recommended.

faq.q: How long does an igniter last?
faq.a: Typically 3-7 years. Silicon nitride igniters last longer than older silicon carbide types.

title: Low Flame Signal / Weak Flame
shortDescription: The flame sensor is detecting a weak or marginal flame signal.
fullDescription: Nine blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate that the flame sensor is reading a low microamp signal, meaning the flame is weak or the sensor is not detecting the flame properly. The furnace may operate intermittently or shut down during a heating cycle.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: A weak flame can indicate incomplete combustion which produces carbon monoxide. Ensure CO detectors are working and call a professional if the problem persists.

causes.cause: Cracked or deteriorated flame sensor insulator

symptoms: Nine LED blinks on control board
Furnace starts but shuts off after a few seconds
Intermittent heating — sometimes works, sometimes does not
Flame appears weak, yellow, or flickering

fixSteps.title: Clean the Flame Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the flame sensor and clean the metal rod with fine emery cloth until shiny. This is the most common fix for low flame signal.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor Insulator
fixSteps.instruction: Check the ceramic insulator around the flame sensor for cracks that could allow the signal to ground out.

fixSteps.title: Observe Flame Color
fixSteps.instruction: Watch the burner flames during operation. They should be mostly blue with small yellow tips. All-yellow or wavering flames indicate a combustion issue.

fixSteps.title: Have Gas Pressure Checked
fixSteps.instruction: A technician can measure the manifold gas pressure to ensure it is within the manufacturer's specification for your furnace.

whenToCallPro: If cleaning the flame sensor does not resolve the issue, or if the flame appears yellow or abnormal.

faq.q: What is a normal flame sensor reading?
faq.a: A healthy flame sensor typically reads 1.5-6 microamps. Below 1 microamp is considered weak and will cause intermittent shutdowns.

faq.q: How often should I clean the flame sensor?
faq.a: Clean it annually as part of routine furnace maintenance. If your furnace frequently shuts down, clean it as needed.

faq.q: Can a dirty flame sensor cause high gas bills?
faq.a: Indirectly, yes. If the furnace frequently shuts down and restarts, it runs inefficiently and uses more gas.

title: Rollout Switch Open
shortDescription: The flame rollout safety switch has tripped, indicating flames are escaping the combustion chamber.
fullDescription: Ten blinks on a Lennox furnace indicate the rollout limit switch has opened. This switch is located near the burners and detects if flames are rolling out of the combustion chamber, which is extremely dangerous. This typically indicates a blocked heat exchanger or cracked heat exchanger.
safetyWarning: CRITICAL SAFETY WARNING: Flame rollout is a serious condition. It can indicate a cracked or blocked heat exchanger, which risks carbon monoxide exposure and fire. Do not attempt to reset the rollout switch and continue operating the furnace. Call a professional immediately.
symptoms: Ten LED blinks on control board
Visible flame outside burner compartment
Scorch marks on furnace cabinet near burners
Strong burning smell
CO detectors may alarm

fixSteps.title: Turn Off the Furnace
fixSteps.instruction: Immediately turn off the furnace at both the power switch and gas supply valve. Do not attempt to reset the rollout switch.

fixSteps.title: Check for CO
fixSteps.instruction: If your carbon monoxide detectors are alarming, evacuate the home immediately and call 911.

fixSteps.title: Check Exhaust Vent
fixSteps.instruction: Visually inspect the exhaust vent pipe for obvious blockages. Do not restart the furnace even if you find and clear a blockage.

fixSteps.title: Call for Professional Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: A licensed HVAC technician must inspect the heat exchanger and determine the cause of flame rollout before the furnace can be safely operated.

whenToCallPro: Immediately. Do not operate the furnace until a professional has inspected and cleared it.

faq.q: What is flame rollout?
faq.a: Flame rollout occurs when flames escape the combustion chamber, often due to a blockage in the heat exchanger or exhaust system. It is a serious fire and CO hazard.

faq.q: Can I just reset the rollout switch?
faq.a: While some rollout switches have a manual reset button, you should never reset it without first having a professional determine and fix the cause. Operating the furnace with an unresolved rollout condition is extremely dangerous.

faq.q: How much does heat exchanger replacement cost?
faq.a: Heat exchanger replacement costs $1,000-$3,000+. If the furnace is old, replacement of the entire furnace may be more cost-effective.

title: Normal Standby / Waiting for Heat Call
shortDescription: The amber LED blinking slowly indicates normal standby operation.
fullDescription: On Lennox furnaces with a multi-color LED, a slow amber blink typically indicates normal standby — the system is powered, all safety checks are passed, and it is waiting for the thermostat to call for heat.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Even in standby, ensure the furnace area is clear of stored items and flammable materials.

causes.cause: Room temperature at or above thermostat setting
Thermostat in off or cool mode

symptoms: Slow amber LED blink
Furnace idle
System responsive to thermostat changes
No error present

fixSteps.title: Verify Thermostat
fixSteps.instruction: Check that the thermostat is set to HEAT and the set temperature is above the current room temperature.

fixSteps.title: Raise Set Point
fixSteps.instruction: Increase the set point 5 degrees above room temp. The furnace should start within 1-2 minutes.

fixSteps.title: Check Thermostat Mode
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the thermostat is not in OFF, COOL, or AUTO mode (if cooling is not needed).

fixSteps.title: Check Programming
fixSteps.instruction: Review thermostat programming and schedules to ensure they match your heating needs.

whenToCallPro: Only if the furnace does not start when the thermostat calls for heat.

faq.q: Is slow amber blinking normal?
faq.a: Yes, it means the system is in normal standby, powered on and ready to heat when called.

faq.q: Why does my Lennox have an amber LED?
faq.a: Many Lennox models use multi-color LEDs — amber for standby, green for normal operation, and red for errors. The color and pattern depend on the model.

faq.q: Should I be concerned about the slow amber blink?
faq.a: No, this is the normal standby indicator. The furnace is operating correctly.

title: Normal Heating — Flame Detected
shortDescription: A fast green blink indicates the furnace is in normal heating operation with flame successfully detected.
fullDescription: On Lennox furnaces with multi-color LEDs, a rapidly blinking green LED indicates normal heating operation. The burners are lit, flame is detected, and the system is running a normal heating cycle.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: During normal operation, ensure nothing is stored on or near the furnace, and that CO detectors are installed and working.

causes.cause: Normal heating operation — not an error
If home is not warming, may have duct or airflow issue
Thermostat set point may need adjustment
Short cycling if green blink is intermittent

symptoms: Fast green LED blink on control board
Warm air from supply vents
Normal furnace operating sounds
Thermostat shows system is running

fixSteps.title: Confirm Normal Operation
fixSteps.instruction: Verify warm air is coming from supply vents throughout the home.

fixSteps.title: Check Airflow
fixSteps.instruction: If heat seems weak, check and replace the air filter. Ensure vents are open.

fixSteps.title: Monitor Cycle Length
fixSteps.instruction: A normal cycle should last 10-20 minutes. Very short or very long cycles may indicate an issue.

fixSteps.title: Check Temperature Rise
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the temperature at a supply vent and the return vent. The difference should be 40-70 degrees F for most furnaces.

whenToCallPro: If the furnace runs but the home does not warm adequately, or if you notice abnormal cycle lengths.

faq.q: Is fast green blinking an error?
faq.a: No, on Lennox furnaces with color LEDs, fast green blinking means the furnace is heating normally.

faq.q: How do I know if my furnace is heating efficiently?
faq.a: Check the temperature rise (supply minus return air temperature). It should match the range on your furnace data plate, typically 40-70 degrees F.

faq.q: Why is my house still cold if the furnace is running?
faq.a: Check for dirty filter, closed vents, duct leaks, or an undersized system. If the furnace is heating but the house is cold, the issue is likely in air distribution.

title: Control Board Failure / Hardware Fault
shortDescription: A continuous red LED indicates an internal control board failure requiring board replacement.
fullDescription: A continuous (non-blinking) red LED on a Lennox furnace indicates an internal hardware fault on the control board itself. The board has detected an irrecoverable error in its own circuitry and cannot operate the furnace.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off the furnace and gas supply. A failed control board cannot manage safety circuits, so the furnace should not be operated until repaired.

causes.cause: Failed control board component (relay, capacitor, processor)
Control board overheated from nearby heat source
Moisture or corrosion damage to the board

symptoms: Continuous red LED — no blinking pattern
Furnace completely non-functional
No response to thermostat commands
May have occurred after power outage or storm

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off the furnace power for 60 seconds and restore. If the continuous red LED returns, the board has failed.

fixSteps.title: Check for Visible Damage
fixSteps.instruction: Look at the control board for burn marks, bulging capacitors, or signs of moisture damage.

fixSteps.title: Check Board Fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the small fuse on the control board is intact. Replace if blown, then test again.

fixSteps.title: Replace Control Board
fixSteps.instruction: Control board replacement is typically a professional repair. The board must match the furnace model exactly and may require configuration.

whenToCallPro: Call a professional for control board replacement. This is not a typical DIY repair.

faq.q: How much does a Lennox control board cost?
faq.a: Lennox control boards typically cost $150-$500 for the part, with total installed cost of $250-$800 depending on the model.

faq.q: Can a power surge damage the control board?
faq.a: Yes, power surges from lightning or utility issues can damage electronic components on the control board. Consider a whole-house surge protector.

faq.q: Is it worth replacing the control board?
faq.a: If the furnace is less than 15 years old, board replacement is usually worthwhile. For older furnaces, compare the repair cost to replacement cost.

title: No Power to Control Board
shortDescription: The diagnostic LED is completely off, indicating no power reaching the control board.
fullDescription: When no LED is visible on the Lennox furnace control board, the board is not receiving electrical power. This could be due to a tripped breaker, blown fuse, open power switch, or failed transformer.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: Turn off the gas supply valve before troubleshooting electrical issues. If you smell gas at any time, leave and call your gas company.
symptoms: No LED visible on control board
Furnace completely unresponsive
Thermostat may be blank
No sounds from furnace at all

fixSteps.title: Check Breaker
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the furnace breaker in your electrical panel. Reset it if tripped.

fixSteps.title: Check Power Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Find the power switch near the furnace (looks like a light switch) and ensure it is ON.

fixSteps.title: Check Board Fuse
fixSteps.instruction: Open the furnace panel and inspect the small fuse on the control board. Replace if blown with the same amperage.

fixSteps.title: Test Transformer
fixSteps.instruction: Measure 24VAC output from the transformer secondary. If absent with 120V present on the primary, the transformer has failed.

whenToCallPro: If the breaker trips repeatedly, or if the transformer or board has failed.

faq.q: Why does the fuse keep blowing?
faq.a: A repeatedly blown board fuse usually indicates a short circuit in the thermostat wiring. Check for damaged wires or a shorted thermostat.

faq.q: Where is the furnace power switch?
faq.a: It is usually mounted on or near the furnace, often at the top of the unit. It resembles a standard light switch.

faq.q: Can a bad thermostat blow the fuse?
faq.a: Yes, a short circuit in the thermostat or its wiring can blow the low-voltage fuse on the furnace control board.

title: Rollout Switch Requires Manual Reset
shortDescription: The flame rollout switch tripped and has a manual reset button that must be pressed.
fullDescription: Eleven blinks on some Lennox models indicate the rollout switch with manual reset has tripped. This switch must be manually reset by pressing its button after the cause of rollout has been determined and fixed.
safetyWarning: Gas furnace safety: If you smell gas, leave immediately and call your gas company.
symptoms: 11-blink LED pattern
System malfunction
No heat or intermittent heat
Unusual sounds or behavior

fixSteps.title: Power Cycle
fixSteps.instruction: Turn off furnace power for 30 seconds, then restore.

fixSteps.title: Check Filter
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the air filter if dirty.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check for loose connections.

fixSteps.title: Call Professional
fixSteps.instruction: If error persists, professional diagnosis is needed.

whenToCallPro: If basic troubleshooting does not resolve the error.

faq.q: What does 11-blink mean?
faq.a: The flame rollout switch tripped and has a manual reset button that must be pressed.

faq.q: Is this serious?
faq.a: It depends on the specific code. Some are minor and some need immediate attention.

faq.q: How much will repair cost?
faq.a: $100-$500 depending on the issue.
